songs.title: Everything Is Made of Tiny Things
songs.hook: Your hand is mostly empty space. Zoom way down and everything—every puppy, every pizza, every star—is built from the same tiny bricks: atoms.

paper.title: The Scattering of α and β Particles by Matter and the Structure of the Atom (Ernest Rutherford, 1911, Philosophical Magazine)

songs.story:
Hold up your hand. It feels solid, but it's mostly empty space. Zoom in far past dust and grains of sand and you reach another country—the world of atoms, the tiny bricks that everything is built from: every mountain, every tree, every puppy, every pizza, every fish in the sea.
Each atom has a tiny, heavy centre called the nucleus, packed with protons and neutrons, with electrons around it. But not in neat little planet-like orbits—that's an old picture. They live in fuzzy clouds of maybe-here, maybe-there. And the nucleus is astonishingly small: blow an atom up to the size of a football stadium and the nucleus is a marble on the fifty-yard line. Almost all of you is elbow room. That mostly-empty atom with a tiny dense core is exactly what Rutherford discovered in 1911, firing particles at gold foil and watching a few bounce straight back.
Change the number of protons and you change the element—hydrogen is light, gold shines, carbon is where life's story flows—the whole periodic table from one simple rule. You, your dog, the Moon: the same handful of building blocks, arranged into different poetry. And next time, up close, the rules of that tiny world get wonderfully strange.

songs.title: The Geometry of Hidden Things
songs.hook: For two hundred years, thermodynamics just balanced its books. It turns out heat was quietly tracing a hidden geometry the whole time.

paper.title: Heat as a gauge connection (Bryan W. Roberts, 2025, arXiv:2503.08753)

songs.story:
For two hundred years, thermodynamics has run on bookkeeping: heat in, work out, the ledger always balancing, entropy only ever climbing. It worked so well that no one thought to ask whether the accounting was quietly hiding something.
It was. Treat heat not as a mere quantity but as a connection—the same kind of geometric object physicists use to describe forces—living over the space of a system's work configurations. When that connection is flat, you recover exactly the familiar thermodynamics: entropy and temperature exist, and heat is simply temperature times the change in entropy. But when it curves, something new surfaces: a thermal echo of the geometric phase from quantum mechanics, a hidden twist that the ordinary heat-and-work ledger can never show.
It's a marble whose shadow can never confess the colour inside it. The quantities we can see—the piston you push, the numbers you read—are only a projection of a richer structure underneath, and the same mathematics may describe a black hole and a steam engine alike. Not a new universe. A new way to see the old one.

songs.title: The Fat Knows When
songs.hook: We treated body fat like a passive savings account. Turns out every fat cell is listening—deciding, moment to moment, whether to hold on or let go.

paper.title: Mitochondrial calcium regulates lipid metabolism by modulating tethering of mitochondria to lipid droplets (Acín Pérez, Assali, Veliova et al., 2026, The EMBO Journal)

songs.story:
For most of us, body fat is a passive savings account: stack it up when there's plenty, spend it down when there isn't. But every fat cell turns out to be actively managed, deciding moment to moment whether to guard its fuel or give it up.
Inside the cell, the mitochondria—the powerhouses that turn fuel into energy—physically dock against droplets of stored fat. Whether they stay docked or pull away is set by a surprising signal: the level of calcium inside the mitochondria themselves, tuned by a molecular exchanger called NCLX. When that calcium rises, the mitochondria change shape and let go of the droplet, and only then can the enzymes move in to burn the fat. When it falls, they hold on, and the fat stays stored.
So burning fat isn't a switch on the fat, or on the engine—it's the conversation between them, a docking and undocking choreographed by a single ion. One little signal decides whether yesterday's dinner is kept or spent. The fat, it turns out, knows when.

songs.title: Hidden Geometry
songs.hook: A property of electrons stayed purely theoretical for twenty years. Then someone found it hiding in ordinary materials.

paper.title: The quantum metric of electrons with spin-momentum locking (Sala, Mercaldo, Domi, Gariglio, Cuoco, Ortix & Caviglia, 2025, Science)

songs.story:
Einstein taught us that space itself can curve. What's less known is that something similar happens on a much smaller stage: the quantum states an electron can occupy inside a crystal have their own geometry, called the quantum metric. It was predicted about twenty years ago and almost impossible to measure directly — until now.
A team at the University of Geneva found a way in. Wherever an electron's spin gets locked to its direction of motion, a trait common at the surface of many everyday materials, that locking generates a measurable quantum metric. They caught it with a clever electrical trick: nonlinear magnetoresistance, a bend in electrical resistance that only this hidden geometry can explain.
The surprise is where they found it. Not in some exotic, one-of-a-kind crystal, but in ordinary oxide interfaces and semiconductors — the everyday stuff circuits are made of. A sliver of curved space, hiding in plain sight.

songs.title: One Water, Two Souls
songs.hook: The most ordinary thing on Earth has been living a double life.

paper.title: Evidence for the generic existence of two local structures in liquid water (Li, Zhong, Zhang, Wang & Zeng, 2026, Nature Physics)

songs.story:
You have poured it, sipped it, and swum in it your whole life — and it turns out water was never just one thing. Physicists suspected for fifty years that liquid water hides two distinct molecular personalities: one crowd packed in tight (high-density water), and one holding itself open like a country road (low-density water), endlessly switching back and forth.
The proof finally came from an unexpected witness. Researchers handed an AI millions of unlabeled snapshots of water molecules — no hints, no answer sheet — and it found the two hidden neighborhoods on its own.
That quiet double life explains one of nature's best tricks: why water gets denser only down to 4 °C and then pushes back, which is why ice floats, lakes freeze from the top down, and life under the surface survives winter. One water, two souls.
